entries.title: NFL draft: Raiders take Indiana QB Fernando Mendoza No. 1
entries.summary: <p>The Raiders did Thursday what most of the football world had suspected they'd do since the NFL season ended, picking Indiana quarterback Fernando Mendoza first overall. NFL commissioner Roger Goodell made the announcement at the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports-media/2026/04/23/nfl-network-charlie-yook-espn-nfl-draft-pittsburgh-daniel-jeremiah-kyle-brandt-peter-schrager-mike-mcquade-dan-patrick-keith-olbermann-sportscenter-the-big-show-miami-university-glenbrook-south-stevenson" target="_blank" >NFL draft</a> in Pittsburgh, just outside the Steelers’ stadium.</p><p>Mendoza becomes the Raiders' next hope to fix a quarterback problem they’ve been unable to solve since Derek Carr left after a Pro Bowl season in 2022. They’ve started seven quarterbacks in the three years since then: Aidan O’Connell, Brian Hoyer, Jimmy Garoppolo, Gardner Minshew, Desmond Ridder, Geno Smith and Kenny Pickett. They’ve gone through four coaches in that same time period: Josh McDaniels, Antonio Pierce, Pete Carroll, and, as of February, former Seahawks offensive coordinator Klint Kubiak.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> asked Caleb Williams to solve an even more dramatic quarterback drought two years ago, picking him first overall. While he set the franchise’s single season passing yards record last year, Williams will have to prove to the Bears this upcoming season that he’s their franchise quarterback. They’ll have the chance to reciprocate; per league rules, they can offer him a contract extension once the season ends.</p><p>While a shoo-in to be the No. 1 pick, Mendoza isn’t as lauded as the USC star was when he entered the 2024 draft. Mendoza, like Miami quarterback Cam Ward a year ago, has the same task as Williams — to justify their No. 1 overall selection and turn around a losing franchise. Williams took an important first step last year, leading the Bears to a playoff win for the first time in 15 years. Amazingly, the Raiders haven’t won a playoff game since the end of the 2002 season, when they eventually lost in the Super Bowl to the Buccaneers. They’ve made the playoffs just twice since.</p><p>Mendoza, who won the Heisman Trophy and the national championship with the Hoosiers this year, watched the draft from Coral Gables, Florida.</p><p>The Bears are slated to draft No. 25 overall Thursday night.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Bears' 1st-round pick Dillon Thieneman checks all boxes at safety, but they had more pressing NFL Draft needs
entries.summary: <p>Even with highly ranked players available at premium positions when <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a> chose No. 25 overall in the first round of the draft Thursday, general manager Ryan Poles took Oregon safety Dillon Thieneman.</p><p>It was a logical choice in the sense that the Bears went into the draft with a vacancy at safety alongside free-agent pickup Coby Bryant, but surprising that they would <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/20/bears-should-count-on-coby-bryant-to-carry-them-at-safety-rather-than-sink-major-draft-capital-into-position" >double up on sizable investments at that position</a> and bypass the chance to capitalize at offensive tackle, defensive tackle or defensive end.</p><p>While there was an early run on offensive tackles with the top six off the board before the Bears’ pick, that frenzy helped shift some compelling options to the Bears at defensive end and defensive tackle. Auburn’s Keldric Faulk, who went 31st to the Titans, and Missouri standout Zion Young were still there at pass rusher. Clemson defensive tackle Peter Woods went 29th to the Chiefs.</p><p>“He was standing out and elevated on the board, so we work laterally and see who else is up there, and was No. 1 on our priority list,” Poles said.</p><p>“This was a really cool opportunity where a need and the best player available fell the way that we needed it to.”</p><p>The Bears went into the draft believing there was a decent chance Thieneman wouldn’t be available at No. 25. Poles said most projections had him going No. 18 to the Vikings. If he made it past that, they felt they had a good chance at him and were interested in trading back a few spots, still thinking they’d land him, but Poles said there wasn’t much demand from the teams behind them.</p><p>“Violent speed” seemed to be the trait that seemed to sway Poles to Thieneman rather than one of the premium positions where the Bears need help.</p><p>“Anytime we’ve seen [an opposing] defense and you feel like it’s suffocating because of their speed, that’s what we’re trying to do,” Poles said.</p><p>Thieneman might become a star, as he was at Oregon, but that isn’t the concern. The Bears’ biggest flaws defensively last season were their struggling pass rush and inability to stop the run, and picking a safety in the first round doesn’t do as much damage on those fronts as a powerhouse defensive lineman.</p><p>“As far as the safety compared to the D-ends compared to the tackles is concerned, we rank them as football players regardless of position,” director of college scouting Breck Ackley said. “We don't overthink that part on the scouting side... When it gets down to [comparing positional needs], that's Poles' main job.”</p><p>The pass rush continues to be a red flag.</p><p>Since Poles took over, the Bears have had the second-fewest sacks in the NFL, including finishing 22nd last season with 35. He has tried, repeatedly, but various moves haven’t gone as planned.</p><p>His biggest move was trading a second-round pick for Montez Sweat in 2023, then extending him on a four-year, $98 million contract, but Sweat hasn’t quite been the force the Bears have needed and they haven’t found him a running mate, either.</p><p>“There’s some opportunity with guys on the board still,” Poles said of the Bears’ upcoming second-round picks at Nos. 57 and 60 overall Friday.</p><p>Defensive end is a tough position to address once it gets that late in the draft, as opposed to safety, where coveted prospects often last into the second or third round. The Bears also have a third-round pick Friday at No. 89 overall.</p><p>The Bears have built what appears to be an impressive secondary with Jaylon Johnson as a shutdown cornerback on the outside, nickel cornerback Kyler Gordon a versatile threat underneath and Bryant and Thieneman on the back end, but that isn’t going to do them much good if they can’t get into the backfield and pressure the opposing quarterback into bad decisions.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: 2026 NFL Draft: Bears pick Oregon safety Dillon Thieneman at No. 25
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> entered the draft looking for an edge rusher Thursday — and didn’t use their first pick on one.</p><p>Rather than take a pass-rusher in a draft known for its depth at the spot, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/23/bears-nfl-draft-raiders-fernando-mendoza-pittsburgh-roger-goodell-indiana-quarterback-heisman-trophy" target="_blank" >Bears</a> turned to the second safety in this year’s draft class with the No. 25 overall pick. Oregon’s Dillon Thieneman will pair with Coby Bryant, the Bears’ biggest free agent addition, in a remarkable outlay of money and draft status for the two safety spots.</p><p>Born in the Indianapolis area, Thieneman played at Purdue for two seasons, where he was named a freshman All-American in 2023 and led the Boilermakers in tackles in 2024. He started all 15 games in his lone year playing for the Ducks, where he was named first-team All-Big-Ten and second-team All-America.</p><p>His four-year rookie contract will pay $19.5 million.</p><p>“I’m very versatile and I’m able to use my speed and play recognition to play fast and play aggressive,” said Thieneman, who attended the draft in Pittsburgh.</p><p>Thieneman’s versatility will fit defensive coordinator Dennis Allen’s preference for defensive backs who can cover all the field and be physical in the run game. Bryant and cornerbacks Jaylon Johnson and Kyler Gordon already fit that bill; Allen planned to cross-train Gordon, a slot corner, at safety last year before he was injured.</p><p>The fact Thieneman doesn’t rush the passer, though, means the Bears are still on the hunt for one of the most important positions in the sport. The team knew it needed to improve its pass rush after finishing second-to-last in pass rush win rate last season — and then failed to add anyone significant in free agency. Defensive end Montez Sweat finished with 10 sacks last season while Austin Booker had 4 ½ sacks in 10 regular season games.</p><p>The Bears last took a Round 1 edge rusher in 2016, when they drafted Leonard Floyd. The team did trade two first-round picks for Raiders edge rusher Khalil Mack in 2018, though.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Adding some speed in the secondary ⚡️<br><br>Welcome to Chicago, <a href="https://twitter.com/DillonThieneman?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">@DillonThieneman</a>! <a href="https://t.co/2UuG2Y6exv">pic.twitter.com/2UuG2Y6exv</a></p>— Chicago Bears (@ChicagoBears) <a href="https://twitter.com/ChicagoBears/status/2047503576348426684?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 24, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Before the draft, the Bears added $10.4 million in salary cap room by doing a standard contract restructure of guard Jonah Jackson and tight end Cole Kmet. By converting their salary in bonuses, the Bears brought their cap number down this year but raised it in 2027.</p><p>Thursday night started when the Raiders drafted Fernando Mendoza first overall, adding yet another quarterback to whom Caleb Williams will be compared — both this upcoming season and into the future.</p><p>Two years after the Bears picked the USC star first overall, the Raiders made Indiana’s Heisman Trophy winner their latest hope to turn around a franchise that hasn’t won a playoff game since 2002. The Bears asked Williams do something similar and watched in Year 2 as he, with the help of new head coach Ben Johnson, led the team to its first postseason victory in 15 years.</p><p>The NFL is a zero sum game, though. Like the Titans’ Cam Ward, last year’s No. 1 overall pick, Mendoza is bound to be compared to Williams — and vice versa. If Mendoza has a better rookie year than the Bears quarterback did — Williams struggled in 2024 and head coach Matt Eberflus was fired — some will wonder whether the Raiders were the team to land the premier young quarterback of his generation. If Mendoza struggles, Williams might look even better by comparison.</p><p>The Bears are hopeful that, after more than 100 years of existence, they’ve landed a star quarterback that can grow with the team for the next decade. They still need to see more, though. While Williams set the franchise’s single season passing yards record last year, he'll have to prove to the Bears this upcoming season that he’s their franchise quarterback. They’ll have the chance to reciprocate; per league rules, they can offer him a contract extension once the season ends.</p><p>While a shoo-in to be the No. 1 pick, Mendoza isn’t as lauded as the USC star was when he entered the 2024 draft. While many thought he’d be the only quarterback taken in the first round Thursday in Pittsburgh, the Rams surprised the NFL by drafting Alabama’s Ty Simpson with the 13<sup>th</sup> overall pick.</p><p>Mendoza, who won the Heisman Trophy and the national championship with the Hoosiers this year, watched the draft from Coral Gables, Florida. Commissioner Roger Goodell announced the selection from a stage outside the Steelers’ stadium.</p><p>Chicago-born Ohio State receiver Carnell Tate was a surprise choice of the Titans at No. 4. Tate, attended Marist before spending the last two years of his high school career at IMG Academy in Bradenton, Florida. Tate, who had 1,872 receiving yards in three seasons in Ohio State, was the second receiver drafted in the top four this past decade.</p><p>The Cowboys moved up from No. 12 to 11 to draft Ohio State safety Caleb Downs. The Dolphins, who picked next, made Alabama’s Kadyn Proctor the third offensive tackle chosen during a four-pick span. The NFC North rival Lions took another, Clemson’s Blake Miller, at No. 17. The run on offensive tackles continued when the Panthers took Georgia’s Monroe Freeling No. 19 and the Steelers took Arizona State’s Max Iheanachor 20<sup>th</sup>. </p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Nick Fuentes' victim says 'no proof' he completed community service, drops criminal charges for civil suit
entries.summary: <p>The criminal case against Nick Fuentes has been dropped months after he was set to complete a deferred prosecution agreement — but the woman he is accused of battering said he didn’t complete the agreement and is now taking him to civil court instead.</p><p>After dropping the criminal case against him fearing it would be strung out even longer than the 18 months that have already passed since the attack, Marla Rose is now suing him in civil court, she told the Sun-Times Thursday.</p><p>While Fuentes attended court via Zoom, Rose was given the chance to take him to criminal trial or drop the charges; she chose the latter after filing a civil court case against him last month. But still, she was “sickened” by what she said were unique circumstances provided for the wealthy far-right influencer.</p><p>“This is not how your average person who pushes a woman down the stairs and breaks six ribs would be treated,” Rose said. “My understanding for months now was that this was all settled, and for whatever reason, it's perfectly reasonable for this defendant to have privileges the average person doesn't. … And it doesn't seem there's anything I can do about that so I’ve decided to move on.”</p><p>Fuentes’ lawyer, Robert Rascia — who is also representing Avondale restaurant Warlord’s former chef, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/01/26/trevor-fleming-warlord-restaurants-crime" target="_blank" ><u>Trevor Fleming</u></a>, in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/food-drink/2026/04/15/hospitality-restaurant-worker-protection-warlord-noma-food-chicago-harassment-chaad-project" target="_blank" ><u>sexual misconduct case</u></a> — didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>The case stems from <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2024/12/06/nick-fuentes-charged-battery" target="_blank" ><u>a confrontation at Fuentes’ Berwyn home in November 2024</u></a>, when he pepper-sprayed and pushed Rose down his front steps after she came to his front door after his address was leaked online. He was charged with misdemeanor battery.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/10/17/nick-fuentes-charlie-kirk-tucker-carlson-infowars-conservative-maga" >As Nick Fuentes’ star rises in wake of Charlie Kirk’s death, he quietly settles battery charge</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/01/16/white-nationalist-nick-fuentes-seal-records-safety-concerns" >White nationalist Nick Fuentes files motion to seal records in criminal case, citing safety concerns</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2024/12/06/nick-fuentes-charged-battery" >Far-right influencer Nick Fuentes charged with battery of Berwyn woman</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The woman went to confront Fuentes after he posted a message on X as the 2024 election results began to favor President Donald Trump. In the post, Fuentes mocked the idea that women have a right to control their own bodies, saying, “Your body, my choice. Forever.”</p><p>As part of the deferred agreement reached in October, Fuentes had signed on to do 75 hours of community service, complete an anger management training, apologize to her in court and pay Rose $635 for her phone.</p><p>While Rose was paid for her phone, she said she lost count of the extensions he was given by judges throughout the case, just to ultimately have “no proof” he completed the community service or anger management class. She was also “disappointed” after being handed an apology letter from Fuentes, which she said likely was written by AI. Rose added she would not have agreed to the deal if she'd known he wouldn't have to apologize in person.</p><p>A motion filed by an attorney working on Rose’s behalf last week argued Fuentes “failed to complete the community service in time” and yet he was seen partying in other states earlier this year.</p><p>In January, Fuentes was captured on video partying with far-right influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate, Clavicular, Sneako and others while listening to Kanye West’s track “Heil Hitler.” Video of this circulated online after being posted by the influencers, and the club apologized for playing the “<a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DTqen1MgQ8b/?utm_source=ig_web_copy_link" target="_blank" ><u>deeply offensive and unacceptable</u></a>” song.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Clavicular, Tristan, Andrew Tate, Sneako and Nick Fuentes seen vibing to the uncensored version of Ye's banned song 'HH' in a Miami club after getting the DJ to play it <br><br> <a href="https://t.co/FgBWTcYFKN">pic.twitter.com/FgBWTcYFKN</a></p>— No Jumper (@nojumper) <a href="https://twitter.com/nojumper/status/2012933758530232603?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">January 18, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>“There was some small hope I had that in doing service for others he might become a more compassionate person,” Rose said.</p><p>“To see the news of him flying off to Miami to dance with the manosphere bros to a song about annihilating Jewish people — it’s hard to put into words, other than to say it reminds me of my disappointment with the Cook County criminal justice system,” Rose, who is Jewish, added.</p><p>Rose said prosecutors were concerned what a trial would do to her given the “many, many” death threats she and her family received in the wake of the case.</p><p>A hearing is scheduled for Tuesday on the civil case, in which she seeks $10,000 from Fuentes for emotional and physical damages, as well as to cover the cost of the security system she said her family purchased following the threats.</p><p>“What I want, ultimately, is for his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/10/17/nick-fuentes-charlie-kirk-tucker-carlson-infowars-conservative-maga" target="_blank" ><u>Groypers</u></a> and his hundreds of thousands of followers to know there are consequences to actions,” she said. “I don't want them to think you can not be held accountable.”</p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: No injuries in Yellow Line derailment
entries.summary: <p>No one was injured after a Yellow Line train derailed Thursday, cutting off service to the Yellow and Purple Lines while diminishing the far north portions of Red Line service, according to the CTA.</p><p>The trains derailed near the Howard station shortly before 5:40 p.m., suspending two lines as well as the Red Line between Howard and Thorndale, a CTA representative said in a statement. No injuries were reported.</p><p>Shuttle buses were running between the closed Red Line stations until 8:40 p.m. as normal service was restored through the evening<b>.</b></p><p>“CTA crews are on-site and working as quickly as possible to resume normal operations due to a report of the derailment of a train approaching the Howard station,” the statement said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

Instead, use today's energy to make long-range plans for the future. Discussions with younger people, friends or members of groups might help you. Instead, take a realistic look at your financial picture and make some long-range plans for the future. Discussions with bosses, parents and authority figures will help you. In fact, someone older might give you advice that ultimately boosts your income or helps you in a practical or financial way.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You can get a lot done today because Lady Luck is with you. The moon is in your sign, dancing beautifully with Mercury, Mars, and Saturn, encouraging you to make sensible, practical plans for the future, especially related to travel, publishing, medicine and the law. Avoid power struggles with partners.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You might have an idea that you feel obsessed about, but you're keeping it to yourself. Don't let this weigh you down. Instead, you can benefit from doing some behind-the-scenes research about financial matters, wills, inheritances, credit cards, insurance issues and shared property. This will be worthwhile.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Avoid an intense encounter with a friend or a member of a group. There is no upside to this. Instead, welcome practical discussions with partners, spouses and friends about making future plans. You might also agree to athletic or competitive activities that will be stimulating.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today people will notice you more than usual. In fact, some people will know personal details about your private life. (Do you need to do some damage control?) Avoid ego battles with parents and bosses. Instead, put your energy into improving your work in a hands-on, practical way.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Avoid squabbles with others today. Instead, make plans for future vacations, fun outings and anything to do with playful activities with kids or sports. Romantic and creative discussions are also favored. Use your mental energy for positive results.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Family discussions can be positive and productive today in a practical way. Explore making home improvements with the help of others. Avoid petty ego battles about money and possessions because they waste your time and rob you of your accomplishments.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is the classic day for emotional conflict with someone. Guilt, jealousy or over-possessiveness might take hold of you. Not good! Instead, you have another choice: Learn something new or make future plans about travel, learning and teaching. A better payoff.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Don't get your belly in a rash about certain issues at work or related to your health today. Chill out. Instead, this is a great day to make practical plans about earnings, cash flow and perhaps the purchase of something. This is where your focus will pay off.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress and author Shirley MacLaine (1934) shares your birthday today. Family and career are your two main loves. You have humanitarian values and the potential for success. This is a slower-paced year. It's OK to rest and rejuvenate your energy. Focus on business and personal relationships with those who are kind, helpful and supportive.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Man stabbed during pre-dawn fight near Buckingham Fountain
entries.summary: <p>A man was in serious condition after being stabbed during a fight Thursday night near Buckingham Fountain in the Loop. </p><p>Around 11:40 p.m., a 23-year-old man and a 27-year-old woman were walking near the fountain in the 500 block of South Columbus Drive when someone approached and an argument turned physical, Chicago police said. </p><p>During the fight, the man was stabbed in the neck with a sharp object, police said. He was taken to Northwestern Memorial Hospital where he was in serious condition. </p><p>The attacker fled north on Lake Shore Drive, officials said. </p><p>Area detectives were investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Don't let gambling be cloak-and-dagger operation in Illinois
entries.summary: <p>I applaud a Robert Herguth and Mitchell Armentrout article from earlier this week — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/21/gov-jb-pritzker-casino-video-poker-illinois-gambling-board-racing-board-chicago-regulators" >“Will transparency fold under Gov. Pritzker’s plan to revamp the Illinois Gambling Board?”</a> It is a valuable and thorough piece of political journalism on an issue that matters to every Illinoisan. Gambling is poised to become the public health crisis of the next generation, and the details of state administration and policy will matter greatly to Illinois' fiscal and physical health for decades.</p><p>The elimination of appointed Gaming/Racing Board members and publicly accessible meetings would be a political disaster for Illinois. It would continue to normalize gambling as an acceptable source of tax revenue, remove valuable opportunities for civic influence on state policy and taint JB Pritzker's image as a defender of democracy.</p><p>The "happy warrior," i.e., Pritzker, should not be making it more difficult for others to fight.</p><p><i>Ben Huizenga, Old Irving Park</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-790000" name="module-790000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Misguided on Medicare </h3><p>I was on private employer-provided insurance for 40 years before going on Medicare. Every paycheck I earned during that period had a bit sliced off to help pay for Medicare and other retirement benefits. Since I retired, I have relied on Medicare and couldn’t be happier.</p><p>Sun-Times reader <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/04/10/teenagers-teen-takeover-chicago-parks-sidewalks-medicare-ballroom-trump" >Mike Cook</a> from Bourbonnais, however, seems to want to drown it in a bathtub, as some Republican zealots recommend for all government programs. He complains that it would require “massive” public subsidies, “difficult legislation” and a “huge bureaucracy.” I’m guessing Mr. Cook knows nothing about Franklin Delano Roosevelt and his struggles. Few good things come easy.</p><p><i>John Hankes, Streeterville</i></p><h3>Focus on Black success</h3><p>Reading Alden Loury's latest column headlined <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/04/11/black-segregation-racism-economic-mobility-chicago-opportunity-insights-alden-loury" >“Segregation, racism stifle economic mobility for many in Black community”</a> makes me glad I moved out of Chicago when I did. According to him, if I were still living in my old Englewood neighborhood, I would be trapped there and unable to engage in upward mobility.</p><p>This opinion piece reeked of hopelessness and self-pity. I think this sullen fatalism can be more dangerous than the racism he says is so omnipresent and omnipotent. It can make some believe their ZIP code determines their destiny.</p><p>Segregation and racism were alive and well when I was born, but those twin evils didn't stop me or most of my peers from doing well in life. Loury even says it's possible as he wrote, "There’s plenty of Black excellence around to prove that it can be done." So the emphasis needs to be on success, not failure. If failure is all you study, there's sure to be more of it.</p><p><i>Joseph H. Brown, Tampa, Florida</i> </p><h3>Iran war </h3><p>Trying to forecast President Donald Trump's next move in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/04/20/how-the-war-in-iran-is-affecting-chicago-colleges-from-canceled-research-to-uncertain-post-grad-plans" >Iran war</a> is a fool's errand. He never anticipated the economic blowback. Now he's like an infant playing whack-a-mole with a huge plastic hammer.</p><p><i>Jim Arneberg, Hoffman Estates</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Indicting the Southern Poverty Law Center is an act of intimidation, retaliation
entries.summary: <p>Democracies are not undone in a single moment. They erode gradually — through toleration of excesses, normalization of intimidation and the quiet shrinking of space for dissent. </p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/southern-poverty-law-center-criminal-investigation-db7fdcf9baa0d1b24b8f1e1f2cebc0be" target="_blank" >federal indictment</a> of the Southern Poverty Law Center should alarm anyone who values justice, public safety and the rule of law because it signals a dangerous escalation in efforts to delegitimize civil rights advocacy and chill lawful participation in our democracy.</p><p>Let us be clear about what this moment represents. This is not a routine exercise of accountability. It is an act of intimidation. </p><p>The Southern Poverty Law Center has spent decades documenting extremism, exposing organized hate and defending vulnerable communities from violence. Its research and litigation have helped law enforcement, policymakers and the public better understand real threats to safety and democracy. </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-cd0000" name="module-cd0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>To target that work through criminal indictment sends a chilling message: Challenge hate or entrenched power, and you may become the target.</p><p>The National Urban League knows this organization well. The Southern Poverty Law Center is a valued member of our Demand Diversity Roundtable, a coalition dedicated to promoting equal opportunity, inclusion and lawful advocacy. Criminalizing or undermining that work is not merely an attack on one organization — it is an assault on the principle that civil society must be free to operate without fear of retaliation for its ideas, research or advocacy.</p><p>Investigations and prosecutions must be grounded in clear and credible evidence of criminal conduct — not ideology, association or lawful advocacy. When the definition of “threat” expands to include organizations working to reduce hate and violence, the net widens dangerously. </p><p>Today, it is the Southern Poverty Law Center. Tomorrow, it could be journalists, researchers, public interest lawyers or community organizations that dare to challenge prevailing narratives. Recognizing this pattern early is essential to preventing escalation and keeping enforcement focused on genuine public safety risks.</p><p>This indictment was not filed in isolation. It reflects a broader and deeply troubling pattern of government overreach and retaliation. </p><p>Investigative reporting has identified hundreds of individuals and institutions subjected to punitive actions that appear politically motivated. Democracy advocates are tracking dozens of cases that raise serious questions about whether the machinery of government is being used to punish dissent rather than uphold the law. These trends demand scrutiny from anyone concerned with checks and balances and the prevention of abuses of power.</p><p>We respect the rule of law. We recognize the importance of addressing real problems such as fraud, corruption and violence. But respect for the law does not require blind acceptance of every government action. </p><p>When enforcement blurs into political targeting, it erodes public trust and normalizes the abuse of power. The legitimacy of our legal system depends not only on outcomes, but on fair, independent processes that are insulated from ideology and partisan agendas.</p><p>History offers sobering lessons. Time and again, efforts to distract from systemic failures have taken the form of attacks on institutions dedicated to justice and accountability. Those efforts never strengthen democracy; they weaken it. They deepen cynicism, discourage civic participation and leave communities less safe, not more. When organizations that expose extremism or defend civil rights are silenced, the harms they work to prevent do not disappear — they grow unchecked.</p><p>This moment is about far more than one indictment or one organization. It is a test of whether we will preserve the space for lawful research, advocacy and accountability that democratic societies require to function. When that space contracts, the rights of all people are threatened, and the foundations that keep communities resilient begin to crack.</p><p>The civil rights community stands united in this moment. An attack on one is an attack on all. We will not be intimidated, and we will not accept a future in which standing up for justice is treated as a criminal act. </p><p>The National Urban League stands shoulder to shoulder with the Southern Poverty Law Center, resolute in the belief that truth-telling, advocacy and the defense of human dignity are not threats to democracy — they are its lifeblood.</p><p>If democracy is to endure, we must draw this line clearly and defend it boldly.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/marc-h-morial" target="_blank" ><i>Marc H. Morial</i></a><i> is president and CEO of the National Urban League</i> <i>and</i> <i>was mayor of New Orleans from 1994 to 2002.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a40000" name="module-a40000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Marc H. Morial

entries.title: Private equity dismantled West Suburban Medical Center and other area hospitals
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/25/west-suburban-medical-center-closing-runs-out-cash-weiss-memorial" target="_blank" >shutdown </a>of West Suburban Medical Center in Oak Park is the <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/news/oak-park-hospital-shutdown-tied-to-private-equity-backed-chain/" target="_blank" >latest disruption in Chicago’s safety-net system</a>. It follows the closure of <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/news/another-former-pipeline-hospital-closes/" target="_blank" >Weiss Memorial Hospital</a> last year and the 2019 shutdown of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2019/4/30/18619458/westlake-hospital-closing-approved-unanimously-by-state-review-board" >Westlake Hospital</a> in Melrose Park. All three hospitals share a common history: Each was owned at one time by Pipeline Health, a private equity-backed hospital chain.</p><p>Pipeline <a class="Link" href="https://investor.tenethealth.com/press-releases/press-release-details/2019/Tenet-Completes-Sale-of-Three-Chicago-Area-Hospitals-to-Pipeline-Health/" target="_blank" >acquired the hospitals</a> in 2019, and its ownership employed financial strategies that weakened these facilities over time, including <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/reports/how-private-equity-raided-safety-net-hospitals-pipeline-health/" target="_blank" >monetizing hospital assets</a>. Today, Weiss and Westlake are closed; West Suburban Medical Center, now owned by other groups, announced in March it was closing temporarily (some outpatient clinics began treating patients last week).</p><p>Chicago’s experience reflects a broader national trend in private equity ownership of hospitals. Private equity has become a significant force in hospital ownership, with approximately <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/pesp-private-equity-hospital-tracker/" target="_blank" >488 hospitals</a> owned by private equity firms as of April 2025, our research shows. That represents about 8.5% of all private, or non-government, hospitals and more than 22% of for-profit hospitals. At the same time, investment activity continues to grow. In 2025 alone, there were more than <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/reports/pe-healthcare-deals-2025-in-review/" target="_blank" >1,000 private equity-backed</a> health care deals across the country. </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b80000" name="module-b80000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>That expansion has coincided with rising financial distress. Private equity firms were involved in 44% of the <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/reports/private-equity-bankruptcy-tracker/" target="_blank" >largest health care bankruptcies</a> in 2025, a disproportionate share given the size of the industry. </p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/reports/the-pillaging-of-steward-health-care/" target="_blank" >collapse of Steward Health Care</a>, a multistate hospital system, illustrates the scale of the problem. When Steward filed for bankruptcy in 2024, it reported more than $9 billion in liabilities, including $6.6 billion in long-term lease obligations tied to real estate deals.</p><p>The financial approach behind these outcomes <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/media_coverage/aft-health-care-how-private-equity-has-looted-our-hospitals/" target="_blank" >follows a familiar model</a>. Private equity firms typically use debt to acquire hospitals and then seek to generate returns within a relatively short time frame. That approach often involves cutting costs, increasing revenue and extracting value through fees and dividends. In some cases, hospitals’ real estate is sold to generate immediate cash, leaving the operating entity responsible for ongoing lease payments.</p><h3>Big returns for investors; debt for hospitals</h3><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/reports/the-pillaging-of-steward-health-care/" target="_blank" >Steward case</a> demonstrates how this model can unfold. A $1.2 billion real estate transaction generated significant returns for investors, while leaving hospitals responsible for substantial rent obligations that continued to weigh on their finances. A similar pattern occurred at <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/news/prospect-safety-net-hospitals-continue-to-struggle-under-the-legacy-of-leonard-greens-past-ownership/" target="_blank" >Prospect Medical Holdings</a>, where investors extracted approximately $658 million in dividends and fees before selling hospital real estate for $1.4 billion through a sale-leaseback transaction. These strategies reduce financial flexibility and make it harder for hospitals to withstand operational or financial disruptions.</p><p>The same pressures are visible in Chicago. Westlake closed following a controversial <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/reports/how-private-equity-raided-safety-net-hospitals-pipeline-health/" target="_blank" >bankruptcy process</a>. <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/news/another-former-pipeline-hospital-closes/" target="_blank" >Weiss shut down</a> after years of financial strain and asset sales (Pipeline sold the hospital to Resilience Healthcare in 2022). West Suburban has now halted services, leaving patients to seek care elsewhere. The details differ in each case, but the trajectory is consistent. Similar pressures have emerged at other private equity-owned hospitals in Chicago, including recent closures of <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/news/apollo-owned-hospitals-close-amid-declining-financial-condition/" target="_blank" >Apollo Global Management-owned Kindred</a> facilities, where mounting debt and an unsustainable financial structure led to layoffs and service consolidation.</p><p>Policymakers across the country have started to respond. As of early 2026,<a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/news/dozens-of-state-bills-propose-addressing-private-equity-in-healthcare/" target="_blank" > lawmakers in 25 states have introduced at least 79 bills</a> addressing private equity ownership in health care. These proposals focus on increasing transparency, strengthening oversight of transactions, and limiting practices that can destabilize providers.</p><p>Illinois lawmakers have introduced measures aimed at increasing oversight of private equity involvement in health care, including <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?GAID=18&DocNum=5301&DocTypeID=HB&LegId=167030&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >House Bill 5301</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?GAID=18&DocNum=3770&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=167082&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >Senate Bill 3770</a>, which would give the attorney general authority to review and potentially block certain transactions. However, both bills are currently stalled in the General Assembly following key legislative deadlines, leaving stronger oversight proposals effectively sidelined, a disservice to people across Illinois who rely on these hospitals for care.</p><p>Another proposal, <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=5000&GAID=18&DocTypeID=HB&LegId=166420&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >House Bill 5000</a>, continues to move forward and would expand notification requirements to the attorney general for health care transactions, including those involving private equity. While that added transparency is a step forward, the bill does not provide the authority to stop harmful deals before they happen.</p><p>House Bill 5301 and Senate Bill 3770 would have provided that authority. Illinois needs this level of oversight <a class="Link" href="https://pestakeholder.org/news/big-step-forward-for-illinois-healthcare-oversight/" target="_blank" >to address a key gap in current review,</a> where financial decisions that affect hospital stability are often not fully evaluated before deals are completed.</p><p>Chicago has already seen the consequences of that gap. The three hospitals connected to the Pipeline Health chain have now experienced closures or service disruptions within seven years. For the communities they served, the effects are immediate: reduced access to care, lost jobs for health care workers and increased strain on the remaining hospitals in the region.</p><p>Private equity will continue to play a role in health care. The question is whether that role will be subject to meaningful oversight. Without stronger safeguards, the pattern seen at Westlake, Weiss and West Suburban is likely to repeat. Illinois has an opportunity to act before more hospitals follow the same path.</p><p><i>Matt Parr is communications director at the Private Equity Stakeholder Project, a nonprofit that researches the impact of private equity investment in health care and other essential services. He lives in Chicago.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-480000" name="module-480000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Matt Parr

entries.title: Dear Abby: Woman's wallet empties as boyfriend's problems pile up
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I am a 59-year-old divorced woman living with my 54-year-old boyfriend. We have lived together for seven months. He purchased the home we live in. Since we have lived here, I have made all the house payments. I also pay for the internet, utilities and all the groceries. We have four cats and five dogs between us. I feel like I'm supporting him. This was supposed to be a partnership in which we share the bills.</p><p>He quit his manufacturing job to go to school for a commercial driver's license. It lasted a month, but then he couldn't find a better-paying job because of things on his driving record from many years ago. After he finally got a job, his employer surprised him with a drug test. He failed because he tested positive for marijuana.</p><p>He said he smokes marijuana to cope with PTSD from his childhood. His employer said he'd rehire him when he got treatment at a clinic, but he started smoking again as soon as he got out! He can't go back to work until he's clean. He can only do a drug test with a male watching to ensure the protocol for the CDL federal requirements. In addition, he has a $1,000-a-month truck payment and is behind on that. He will probably lose the truck and expect to drive my car. I refuse to allow him to run my car into the ground.</p><p>I feel like I'm trapped into supporting this man who can't seem to keep a job. What should I do? — <i>COULD KICK MYSELF IN ILLINOIS</i></p><p><b>DEAR COULD KICK:</b> What you must do is understand that you cannot fix what is wrong with this man. Fortunately, you are not on the hook for the mortgage payments. Consider the monies you have paid to be tuition for some important life lessons. He is a loser who will never pull his own weight. Get away from him.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My boyfriend of five years refuses to give me a key to his place. It feels like he doesn't want to fully commit and take the relationship to the next level. I'm tired of wasting my time, and I really want to break up. He is begging me to stay and give him more time, but I see no reason to.</p><p>He blames his previous marriage on his inability to fully commit and trust. He has been caught twice dealing with other females, and at this point, I see no reason to continue this relationship. I am 43, and he's 45. Should I continue giving him more time or leave him where I found him? — <i>OVERDUE IN WASHINGTON, DC.</i></p><p><b>DEAR OVERDUE:</b> Five years is a long time to invest in a relationship that hasn't moved forward, backward or in any direction. Because your boyfriend has trust issues, he should have spent some time and money on resolving them. If you're fishing for a husband, throw this one back.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For everything you need to know about wedding planning, order "How to Have a Lovely Wedding." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Wedding Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Some Chicagoans aren't paying federal taxes in protest of Trump policies
entries.summary: <p>When Chicago attorney Rachel Cohen<b> </b>sat down to do her taxes<b> </b>earlier this year,<b> </b>she thought about the half dozen times she had been tear-gassed or struck with pepper pellets at protests against federal deportation tactics.</p><p>“I was thinking about my willingness to be hit with chemical weapons,” Cohen said. “And I couldn't square giving the federal government $10,000 with being willing to get arrested or put my body on the line.”</p><p>Cohen, an independent contract employee and Harvard-educated attorney, decided to file but not pay her federal taxes, instead putting the owed amount into a high-yield savings account and paying only her state taxes instead.</p><p>Protesting has long involved picketing, marches and even hunger strikes. But some Chicagoans, angered over the Trump administration’s tactics, are trying a different form of civil disobedience: not paying their federal taxes.</p><p>“War tax resistance” organizers say they’re seeing a barrage of new interest in their longstanding movement by people appalled at the federal government’s aggressive deportation efforts, U.S. support for Israel in the war in Gaza, and the U.S.-Israel war against Iran.</p><p>“The watershed moment was the invasion of Gaza with mostly U.S.-funded weapons,” said Lincoln Rice, a Milwaukee organizer with the National War Tax Resistance Coordinating Committee who hasn’t paid most of his federal taxes since 1998. “It's been waves growing on waves. This definitely is our busiest tax season that I've witnessed.”</p><p>The group provides training on tax resistance and its consequences, measures interest by how many people go to their website or follow their social media. In 2018, the group had 157 followers on Instagram. Today, it has 42,000. Before the war in Gaza, the website had about 40,000 unique visitors a year, compared with 400,000 last year and 300,000 this year, Rice said.</p><p>In trainings, the group goes over the two main ways to protest taxes. It refers to one as “simple living,” when people lower their income by so much that they won’t owe federal taxes — roughly $16,000 for an individual<b>.</b></p><p>“Any other way where you're resisting,” such as not paying your taxes at all, Rice said, “is always illegal. It's a form of civil disobedience. We want to make sure that's very clear to people.”</p><p>The consequences of not paying federal taxes can range from threatening letters from the Internal Revenue Service,<b> </b>to a monthly fine of up to 1% of the taxes owed. Consequences for not filing a federal tax return are worse, with a 5% fine attached. In extreme cases, tax protesters could face wage garnishment, property seizures or prison time, though criminal prosecutions are rare, according to University of Chicago law professor David Weisbach.</p><p>“They don't often do that, but they can. And so it's a form of civil disobedience that comes with all the consequences of civil disobedience, which is that you are subject to legal sanctions, and they can be quite severe,”<b> </b>Weisbach said. “It's certainly one way of protesting, but it's a risky way, and it could be a very, very costly way.”</p><h3>Tax protest at ‘dire moment’</h3><p>Cohen said she weighed the risks before deciding that, even though she is a lawyer, she would break the law and then publicize having done so on social media.</p><p>“I think that, particularly as a Harvard-educated white woman with a social media platform, it's really important that I do my research before I go and urge people to do something or, in this case, tell them about something that I'm doing because I've been very careful not to encourage other people to do tax resistance but to document why I'm doing it and the thought process,” she said.</p><p>Cohen has gained prominence for her pushback against President Donald Trump and his deportation campaign in the Chicago area.<b> </b>Her tax protest posts have gone viral, perhaps increasing the likelihood of being targeted by the IRS. For her, in an extreme case, that could mean disbarment, or losing the condo that she worked hard to own.</p><p>Still, Cohen said she feels it’s important to broadcast her choice.<b> </b></p><p>“A sentiment that I think is widely held is just kind of the question of why people aren't doing more. And I think we end up kind of gaslighting ourselves into thinking that the moment is not as dire as it is because, if it was so dire, people would be doing more.</p><p>“But I am somebody that spent my entire life working to get into these specific spaces, and I think that we are at such a dire moment that I am willing to give those things up. And, if you're wondering how dire the moment is, because it seems like nobody is reacting appropriately, I want people to know: I agree with you.”</p><p>Weisbach, who worked for the Treasury Department in the 1990s, said tax protests haven’t made much of a dent in the federal budget, half of which is earmarked for mandatory programs, like Medicare, Medicaid and Social Security. About 13% of the budget goes toward military spending, though that figure rises when you take into account interest payments on war borrowing and health care costs for veterans, according to the <u>Costs of War project</u> at Brown University.</p><p>Weisbach said the tax protest movement isn’t necessarily about making a dent in the federal budget.</p><p>“The whole point of civil disobedience is to change people's views about the matter,” he said. “Martin Luther King, that's what he did. They march on a bridge, they break the law, the law was unjust, and they changed people's views about race. But did he directly change a law? Not so much. He changed people's views, which caused laws to change.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-600000" name="image-600000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2192e04/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F92%2Fe340024e4cff8a7d09b4af222e32%2Fimg-9799.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/459f4a3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F92%2Fe340024e4cff8a7d09b4af222e32%2Fimg-9799.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b98e9a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F92%2Fe340024e4cff8a7d09b4af222e32%2Fimg-9799.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Em Jacoby, 40, who runs a small tax resistance organization from her Northwest Side home. She decided for the first time this year to stop paying federal taxes as a form of protest against the Trump administration." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b98e9a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F92%2Fe340024e4cff8a7d09b4af222e32%2Fimg-9799.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3ecd575/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F92%2Fe340024e4cff8a7d09b4af222e32%2Fimg-9799.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b98e9a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2F92%2Fe340024e4cff8a7d09b4af222e32%2Fimg-9799.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Em Jacoby, 40, runs a small tax resistance organization from her Northwest Side home. She decided for the first time this year to stop paying federal taxes as a form of protest against the Trump administration.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Budding resistance in Logan Square </b></h3><p>At her home in Logan Square, Em Jacoby said she fields a couple of calls a week from people engaged in tax resistance. She runs a longstanding organization that helps protesters invest the money they would have paid in federal taxes. When the money earns interest, her group doles out grants to community organizations.</p><p>“Our Chicago resisters have been reaching out to increase their deposits,” Jacoby said. “Some of them were dormant for quite a while, and now they're reaching out and saying, ‘I'm really upset. I want to put more money in.’ ”</p><p>Last spring, Jacoby, who had never been a tax resister before, took over for an older woman who ran the group for 40 years.</p><p>“She trained me when she was still able to, and I literally picked up the documents from her really quaint, quirky apartment in Boston,” Jacoby said.</p><p>The group’s board members are in their 70s and 80s.<b> </b>Many in the movement joined to protest the war in Vietnam or, later, U.S. foreign policy in Iraq.</p><p>Rice joined after he saw then-Secretary of State Madeleine Albright in a “60 Minutes” interview. Reports at the time estimated that up to half a million Iraqi children had died due to sanctions on the country, and Albright was asked whether that cost was worth the effort to thwart Saddam Hussein. She responded: “This is a very hard choice but… we think the price is worth it.”</p><p>“That was an eye-opening moment that… every administration is doing something that's so appalling that I cannot, in good conscience, see myself paying and supporting it,” Rice said.</p><p>War tax resistance started long before the internet — in people’s living rooms, where you had to know someone who was already doing it in order to get involved.</p><p>Jacoby has created an Instagram account in hopes of appealing to a new generation.</p><p>“This is our first try at social media, but we have been around since 1968,” a pinned post reads. “Stay tuned if you long for good trouble.”</p><p>This year, for the first time, Jacoby said she’s not paying federal taxes — in protest against the federal government’s actions abroad. She said her breaking point came after her young child<b> </b>had seen reports of bombing in Gaza, where more than 20,000 children have been killed, and had a pointed question:</p><p>“Hearing my 7-year-old say, ‘We're safe from the bombing here, aren't we, Mom?’ Just holding that and also knowing how much privilege it is that she can say that, I think that's what moves me.”</p>
entries.author: Mariah Woelfel | WBEZ

entries.title: ‘This was built for you.’ West Garfield Park celebrates nearly $50 million wellness center
entries.summary: <p>Marquis Pitts was on the verge of breaking down as he shared his story with about 300 people packed onto a basketball court in the new Sankofa Village Wellness Center in West Garfield Park.</p><p>He got tired of opening his phone and seeing yet another person he grew up with dead, “another rest in peace,” he said Thursday. The senseless killing has to stop, he pleaded.</p><p>“That’s what this wellness village is about. This is about saving lives,” said Pitts, 28, who has spent his entire life in this West Side community.</p><p>“Don't let this become something we have,” he cautioned. “Make it something we use because this was built for you.”</p><p>Like many in the crowd, Pitts was there to celebrate the opening of Sankofa — and heal. At 60,000 square feet, around the size of a football field, Sankofa beams along Madison Street, a once-bustling corridor devastated by white flight and the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wttw.com/chicago-stories/when-the-west-side-burned/remembering-the-1968-riots" target="_blank" ><u>1968 riots</u></a> that has not seen big investment in decades. At the new center, residents can see a doctor or a dentist, play in the indoor gym, stroll on a walking track, or get help finding a job.</p><p>It was years in the making and created with community input to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2025/11/20/death-gap-racial-life-expectancy-gap-west-garfield-park-heart-disease-cancer-homicide-opioid-overdose-chicago-west-side-loop" target="_blank" ><u>improve health disparities in West Garfield Park.</u></a> In 2023, residents in this mostly Black neighborhood were expected to live until 67 years old — the lowest life expectancy in Chicago — compared to 87 in the Loop, where the majority of residents are white.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicagohealthatlas.org/indicators/VRLE?tab=map" target="_blank" ><u>New Chicago public health data</u></a> for 2024 shows progress. Residents in West Garfield Park can now expect to live on average about three years longer, until they’re 70 years old. This community now ranks third for having the lowest life expectancy, with Fuller Park on the South Side <a class="Link" href="https://chicagohealthatlas.org/indicators/VRLE?tab=table" target="_blank" ><u>ranking first</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28668843"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28668843/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>Most Chicagoans are expected to live longer. A new <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/content/dam/city/depts/cdph/policy_planning/Board_of_Health/2026/BOH-April-2026-Presentation-ax.mm.em.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago health department presentation</u></a> said life expectancy across the city was “at an all-time high” of <a class="Link" href="https://chicagohealthatlas.org/indicators/VRLE?tab=chart" target="_blank" ><u>about 80 years</u></a> in 2024, up slightly from 2023, with the largest increases on the South and West sides.</p><p>Since the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, average life expectancy regardless of race has jumped about four years, the new data shows. Black Chicagoans can expect to live on average to 73, white residents to 82, Latino residents to 83, and Asian residents to 87.</p><p>Black Chicagoans have made the most gains since the pandemic, adding almost six years to their lives from 2020 to 2024, followed by Latinos, Asians and white residents. Average life expectancy for Asians and Latinos is still lower than before the pandemic.</p><p>Chronic disease fueled by heart disease is still the biggest driver of the so-called death gap between Black Chicagoans and other races, the city’s presentation said. But homicides and opioid overdoses contributed to fewer deaths. A spokesperson for the Chicago public health department declined an interview request.</p><p>Overall, a 20-year life expectancy gap remains in the city. In the majority Black neighborhood of Fuller Park, residents can expect to live on average to 68, while those in the Loop can continue to expect to live the longest, to 88.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" data-align-center> <a class="AnchorLink" id="gallery-530001" name="gallery-530001"></a> <div> <div class="Carousel-slides"> <div class="Carousel-slide"> <div class="CarouselSlide" > <div class="CarouselSlide-media"> <picture data-crop="large"> <source type="image/webp" width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5e0a509/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x4045+0+19/resize/840x555!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F78%2F364f273c4d2ba886797b04e3f646%2Fsankofaopening-260424-11.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b4a8e42/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x4045+0+19/resize/1680x1110!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F78%2F364f273c4d2ba886797b04e3f646%2Fsankofaopening-260424-11.jpg 2x"/> <source width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8a1ce73/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x4045+0+19/resize/840x555!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F78%2F364f273c4d2ba886797b04e3f646%2Fsankofaopening-260424-11.jpg"/> <img class="Image" alt="A Mural at the Sankofa Village Wellness Center at 4305 W.
