aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>During the Sankofa Village ceremony, Ayesha Jaco, executive director of West Side United, one of several organizations that helped create the wellness center, read a letter she wrote to her late grandmother and a late friend who was deeply involved in Sankofa. Jaco described how much people can do at the center without having to leave the neighborhood, how her grandmother and friend would love the bright art on the wall, how an exhibition about the Great Migration would be in the cafe.</p><p>“I just wanted you to know that after all this time, positive change came to the neighborhood,” Jaco said. “I just wish you were around to see it, but I know you’re here with us.”</p><p>Sankofa, at nearly $50 million, is part of a bigger plan to create a walkable village with other nearby investments, including the <a class="Link" href="https://blockclubchicago.org/2025/09/10/maafa-redemption-project-breaks-ground-on-8-million-community-center-for-west-siders/" target="_blank" ><u>MAAFA Center for Arts and Activism</u></a> and the K Entrepreneurship Development Hub.</p><p>The idea is to keep money from leaving the area, ultimately improving conditions that contribute to how long a person can expect to live. Now, many residents leave West Garfield Park for basic resources, from grocery shopping in nearby suburbs to exercising at parks they drive to because it’s not safe to take a walk close to home.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/chicagos-death-gap" target="_blank" ><u>WBEZ documented this and more</u></a> in a series of stories about how the life expectancy gap in West Garfield Park affects generations of families and the fabric of the community. Sankofa is one of <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2026/02/27/growing-healthy-food-to-narrow-chicagos-20-year-life-expectancy-gap-if-we-eat-better-we-do-better" target="_blank" ><u>several community efforts</u></a> to narrow the life expectancy gap.</p><p><i>Kristen Schorsch covers the health of the region for WBEZ. </i><br></p>
entries.author: Kristen Schorsch | WBEZ

entries.title: NFL Draft: Best players available for the Bears to pick Day 2
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> general manager Ryan Poles is being more aggressive than ever in highlighting exactly the kind of player that fits the Bears in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/23/2026-nfl-draft-bears-pick-oregon-s-dillon-thieneman-at-no-25" target="_blank" >NFL Draft</a>. If they don’t, he said, they’re off his draft list.</p><p>In the moments after the first round ended Thursday night, Poles looked at those remaining players and thought, for a moment, that Friday's crop looked thin. He reminded himself, though, that these things tend to work out.</p><p>“It feels really thin, but that's a good thing,” Poles said. “I think it's a really good things because we're really selective of who we want. Now, there's some historical numbers that I have that makes me feel confident that there's gonna be guys that we like still available to us. Thankfully the numbers are high enough where I'll be able to sleep a little bit [Thursday] night."</p><p>Here are players at the Bears’ positions of need — edge rusher, defensive tackle, offensive tackle, cornerback and wide receiver — that could be available Friday when the Bears pick at No. 57 and 60 in Round 2:</p><h3>Edge rusher</h3><p>Clemson’s <b>T.J. Parker,</b> Texas A&M’s <b>Cashius Howell</b> and Missouri’s <b>Zion Young</b> highlight the best remaining defensive ends. They all have their flaws — Parker had five sacks last year and Howell has short arms — but that’s easier to overlook on Day 2 and 3 of the draft. Other edge rushers who will be picked Friday include Oklahoma’s <b>R. Mason Thomas</b> and Illinois’ <b>Gabe Jacas</b>.</p><h3>Defensive tackle</h3><p>Ohio State’s <b>Kayden McDonald</b> is only 21 and probably the best run-stuffing defensive tackle in the draft. Texas Tech’s <b>Lee Hunter</b> can stop the run but is a more skilled pass-rusher than McDonald. NFL Network ranks McDonald No.33 and Hunter No. 41. Georgia's <b>Christen Miller</b> and Missouri’s <b>Chris McClellan</b> are other candidates to be picked.</p><h3>Offensive tackle</h3><p>The Bears could find their next starting left tackle just down Green Bay Road, where Northwestern’s <b>Caleb Tiernan</b> toiled his whole college career. At 6-7, 323 pounds, he’s got a similar build to that of Ozzy Trapilo, who is expected to miss most — if not all — the season with a patellar tendon injury.<br>Mulleted Iowa redhead <b>Gennings Dunker</b>, who can play all five spots on the line, is available, too.</p><h3>Cornerback</h3><p>If the Bears want to find someone to challenge Tyrique Stevenson for the starting job, cornerback is the deepest position left on the board. NFL Network top-100 cornerbacks still available include the Tennessee duo of <b>Colton Hood</b> and <b>Jermod McCoy</b>, South Carolina’s duo of <b>Brandon Cisse</b> and <b>Jalon Kilgore</b>, Clemson's <b>Avieon Terell, </b>Indiana’s <b>D’Angelo Ponds</b>, Miami’s <b>Keionte Scott</b>, Georgia’s <b>Daylen Everette</b>, Washington’s <b>Tacario Davis</b>, Arkansas’ <b>Julian Neal</b>, Arizona State’s <b>Keith Abney II</b> and Florida’s <b>Devin Moore</b>.</p><h3>Wide receiver</h3><p>The Bears can improve upon Kalif Raymond as their third wide receiver. Washington’s <b>Denzel Boston</b>, Clemson’s <b>Antonio Williams</b> and Notre Dame’s <b>Malachi Fields</b> are atop the list of best remaining wideouts.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Berwyn Eagles Club to stop hosting wrestling after fight
entries.summary: <p>The operator of the iconic indie wrestling venue Berwyn Eagles Club says it will stop hosting shows after more than 20 years — news that has left members of Chicago’s wrestling community stunned.</p><p>The final events this weekend include a sold-out show hosted by <a class="Link" href="https://aawpro.com/" target="_blank" ><u>AAW Pro Wrestling</u></a> on Friday and a lucha extravaganza from <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/allstarluchalibrewrestling/" target="_blank" ><u>All Star Lucha Libre</u></a> on Saturday.</p><p>Wrestling fans say that the Eagles Club offered budget-friendly prices and free parking. Some of the world’s biggest wrestling stars have shed blood, sweat and tears in the club, making it a cornerstone venue for a sport that combines athleticism, entertainment and sometimes raw brutality.</p><p>The plans to end wrestling at the venue come after a fight broke out between an unnamed fan and a wrestler, Mads Krule Krügger, at a March 28 Ruthless Pro Wrestling show. Videos posted to <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/juicefreak247/status/2038201492163670247" target="_blank" ><u>social media</u></a> showed wrestlers and attendees punching and kicking the fan.</p><p>The videos sparked misleading reports from some dirt sheets that cover the industry that someone had been stabbed. But according to a police report obtained by the Sun-Times, Berwyn officers found that no stabbing occurred.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a10000" name="image-a10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27e2ed2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2Fa0%2F5d413ab749a5a4ac9dc3bb8f73fd%2Fberwyneagles-260424-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bb59de3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2Fa0%2F5d413ab749a5a4ac9dc3bb8f73fd%2Fberwyneagles-260424-6.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/27e2ed2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F89%2Fa0%2F5d413ab749a5a4ac9dc3bb8f73fd%2Fberwyneagles-260424-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Wrestling fans say that the Eagles Club offered budget-friendly prices and free parking. The storied venue will stop hosting wrestling shows after more than 20 years. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Chuck Marose Sr., who runs the club with his wife, confirmed over the phone that all wrestling events end this weekend. He declined an interview, but said he and his fellow trustees would be “willing” to speak in the future. </p><p>Marose Sr. is listed as a trustee and the secretary of the nonprofit Fraternal Order of Eagles, according to <a class="Link" href="https://projects.propublica.org/nonprofits/organizations/362102094/202523219349328932/full" target="_blank" ><u>2024 tax filings</u></a>. The Fraternal Order of Eagles owns the property, according to the Cook County Recorder of Deeds.</p><p>Word of the closure quickly circulated among promoters, who create, market and produce wrestling events. Game Changer Wrestling <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/GCWrestling_/status/2042326205236388136" target="_blank" ><u>posted on X</u></a> that the venue informed them that all future events would be canceled.</p><p>Donations from the wrestling community have poured into a <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/support-berwyn-eagles-club-chuck-marose-sr" target="_blank" ><u>GoFundMe</u></a> page set up by a fan on behalf of the Berwyn Eagles Club, citing “an incident beyond their control,” in which “the venue lost its license and is no longer allowed to host wrestling shows.”</p><p>“The loss not only impacts the club's day-to-day operations but also puts its long-term future at risk as they search for new ways to sustain the business,” the description continues. As of press time, the <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/support-berwyn-eagles-club-chuck-marose-sr" target="_blank" ><u>GoFundMe</u></a> has raised nearly $1,400 of its $3,000 goal. Beyond wrestling, the Berwyn Eagles club also hosts bingo nights, Queen of Hearts games and community and fraternal events.</p><p>But exactly why the club is ceasing all wrestling events remains unclear. Berwyn’s city attorney and chief of police confirmed with a reporter that the venue’s entertainment license — necessary for hosting wrestling shows — has not been revoked as a result of the events on March 28.</p><p>Berwyn chief of police Michael Fellows said that the Berwyn Eagles Club pays for one officer during wrestling events. After the brawl, the department recommended increasing the total number of on-site officers to four.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c70000" name="image-c70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bcd0dcd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7294x4094+0+871/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F32%2F3ffe61b64eebb3240bc575abec31%2Fringoffire2025-586.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3d5124b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7294x4094+0+871/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F32%2F3ffe61b64eebb3240bc575abec31%2Fringoffire2025-586.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e9c4009/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7294x4094+0+871/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F32%2F3ffe61b64eebb3240bc575abec31%2Fringoffire2025-586.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago natives and professional wrestlers Mustafa Ali and Joe Alonzo face off at an AAW Pro Wrestling show in 2025. data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e9c4009/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7294x4094+0+871/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc4%2F32%2F3ffe61b64eebb3240bc575abec31%2Fringoffire2025-586.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago natives and professional wrestlers Mustafa Ali and Joe Alonzo face off at an AAW Pro Wrestling show in 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Karl Schroepfer/Karl Alan Media</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Wrestling’s toxic fan culture</b></h3><p>While what goes on in the ring is often scripted, what happened on March 28 at the club was not a part of the show.</p><p>Rickey Shane Page, co-owner of Ruthless Pro Wrestling, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/RickeyShanePage/status/2044187774971248920?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw%7Ctwcamp%5Etweetembed%7Ctwterm%5E2044187774971248920%7Ctwgr%5Ee3714485809e26791b1a1e3960df4f3ceb2312f3%7Ctwcon%5Es1_&ref_url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.tpww.net%2F2026%2F04%2Fricky-shane-page-issues-statement-on-fake-fan-stabbing-incident-at-3-28-ruthless-pro-wrestling-event-any-claims-of-it-being-a-work-are-patently-false%2F" target="_blank" ><u>posted</u></a> a statement to X: “Any implication that it was somehow a ‘work’ or ‘storyline’” – a planned event – “is patently false.”</p><p>The evening’s altercation escalated when the main event was over. Fans and wrestlers typically hang out after hours for meet-and-greets, merch sales and networking.</p><p>The police report states that the fan in question “started causing problems early in the night,” according to a wrestling manager.</p><p>Another fan, Loren Rosenberg, who was there on the night of the altercation, said the alleged instigator is a “known problem” in local wrestling circles. While he doesn’t know the fan’s name, Rosenberg recalls a night when this same person “was obviously inebriated” and yelled homophobic slurs at a queer wrestler.</p><p>Wrestler harassment, including female wrestlers, has been <a class="Link" href="https://sports.yahoo.com/articles/elayna-black-calls-fan-allegedly-221220508.html" target="_blank" ><u>an issue in the industry</u></a>.</p><p>“Wrestling should be for everyone, but we can't make others feel uncomfortable,” Rosenberg said. “Unfortunately, it may take a few people just to ruin what is a very special venue.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-170000" name="image-170000"></a> <picture data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Maggie Lee, who is currently signed with Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (also known as TNA), is a familiar face within Chicago’s independent wrestling scene.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Karl Schroepfer/Karl Alan Media</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>The loss of an “accessible” and “affordable” venue</b></h3><p>As the Berwyn club winds down wrestling events, dozens of local and national promotions, including longtime venue residents AAW Pro Wrestling, have been forced to cancel their events or find new sites for shows. Wrestling shows are typically booked out months, if not years, in advance.</p><p>Trent Zuberi, a producer for AAW, said that his company’s co-owner, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/aawdannydaniels" target="_blank" ><u>Danny Daniels</u></a>, was first informed by Marose Sr. of the sudden cancellations.</p><p>AAW launched its first-ever show at the Eagles Club in 2004, and its last show at the venue is happening on Friday. Zuberi said his group has booked venues such as 115 Bourbon Street in Marionette Park for future events.</p><p>But for their last show at Berwyn Eagles Club, “we're gonna go out in style,” Zuberi said. “We’ve got all titles on the line. All of our champions are booked. We got some surprises booked.”</p><p>Matt Riddle, who was formerly tag team partners with WWE’s Randy Orton, will be on the card at Friday’s show, too.</p><p>“The biggest part of the loss is that it's a very accessible and affordable venue,” Zuberi adds, with general admission or standing room tickets typically starting at $20. At its peak, AAW could afford to move out of the Berwyn Eagles Club.</p><p>But as more promotions hit the scene, the group scaled back and returned to the club full-time in 2021. </p><p>“But we made the best of it because we're able to bring our prices down,” Zuberi said. “It's a working-class neighborhood, it's more affordable for people, easy to get to, not far from the highway. It was an accessible venue for the promoters as far as being able to schedule, rent and store things there.</p><p>“That's what people have lost,” he continued. “Now we've lost a place where the biggest companies can pack a house, <i>and</i> the guys that just started a month ago can begin their dreams.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/73567c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6571x3688+0+785/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fea%2F5c%2Fe3a3a7df4ab093d4836533001a7f%2Fcrushanddestroy-321.jpeg" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago wrestler Joe Alonzos shaves a young fan’s head at a past All American Wrestling Pro show.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Karl Schroepfer/Karl Alan Media</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>The end of an era</b></h3><p>High-flying luchadores <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/penta_zero_miedo/" target="_blank" ><u>Penta</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/reyfenixmx/" target="_blank" ><u>Rey Fénix</u></a>, who are dominating in the big leagues, stopped in Berwyn for matches years ago. Current WWE champion Becky Lynch wrestled as Rebecca Knox for the all-women’s promotion <a class="Link" href="http://www.shimmerwrestling.com/p/results.html" target="_blank" ><u>SHIMMER</u></a> at the club, too.</p><p>Homegrown wrestlers were devastated to hear the news, too. Robert “Ego” Anthony <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/Egos1313" target="_blank" ><u>posted a message</u></a> about the Eagles Club’s last AAW show to X: “I’m sad and proud to say I’ll be there for the start and the ending. Let’s send it out right.”</p><p>Matt Knicks, who’s wrestling at the All Star show on Saturday, referred to the “legendary” club as a “wrestling institution” <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DXFDZnpDeGk/?hl=en&img_index=1" target="_blank" ><u>on Instagram</u></a>. </p><p>“This affects so many different circles,” Rosenberg said, noting that several wrestling schools also operated out of the space. “That was kind of the place where it's like, if you made it to Berwyn Eagles, you can make it anywhere.”</p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Obamacare premiums in Illinois rose sharply but not as much as expected
entries.summary: <p>Monthly payments for health insurance through the Affordable Care Act, commonly known as Obamacare, did not increase by as much this year as Illinois officials had initially expected after the expiration of federal tax credits that made coverage significantly more affordable.</p><p>In Illinois, officials had estimated that enrollees would see an average increase of 78% in their monthly premiums this year. But nearly three months after open enrollment ended, Illinois officials now say premiums only rose by about 25%.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/09/aca-obamacare-tax-credit-congress-illinois-decrease-enrollment" target="_blank" ><u>drop-off in enrollees</u></a> also has not been as steep as expected, at about 4%. In 2025, a record 465,985 individuals across the state signed up for the ACA. That number decreased this year to 448,568 enrollees, according to state data.</p><p>Still, medical providers in the Chicago area say it’s too soon to see the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/18/obamacare-aca-tax-credits-expiring-premiums-congress-january-spike-health-care-insurance" target="_blank" ><u>full impact of the expired subsidies</u></a>, particularly because large numbers of people were auto-enrolled in coverage who could still drop off. They also worry that increases in health care costs in general are adding to the increasing number of uninsured patients they are seeing.</p><p>Nearly 43% of people in Illinois on the ACA were automatically re-enrolled. Enrollees typically have a grace period for keeping insurance even if they do not make the monthly payment, but those unpaid plans are typically canceled 90 days after enrollment.</p><p>Matthew McGough, an ACA policy analyst for KFF, which conducts health policy research and polling, said data on the number of people dropped from the program for lack of payment will come out later this year.</p><p>“That’ll be our best indication of people who are outside of the grace period and didn’t have their coverage effectuated," McGough said. “We’re waiting for that data to be able to, I think, start to really see how this picture is shaping up.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/09/aca-obamacare-tax-credit-congress-illinois-decrease-enrollment" >Illinois extends ACA enrollment after fewer people sign up for health insurance plans</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/18/obamacare-aca-tax-credits-expiring-premiums-congress-january-spike-health-care-insurance" >Chicagoans brace for rising Obamacare costs or no coverage with Congress poised to end subsidies</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/10/24/expiring-aca-obamacare-tax-credits-federal-government-shutdown-chicago" >What to know about expiring Obamacare tax credits</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Get Covered Illinois, the state-run ACA marketplace, said one reason monthly premiums didn’t rise dramatically is because the state was able to implement a strategy called “premium alignment.” That’s a tool that stabilizes the sticker price insurers charge for a premium, McGough said.</p><p>The household ACA monthly average premium is $328, up from $260 a year, according to state data. But if people buying coverage on the ACA exchange hadn’t adjusted their plans at all this year, the state expected the monthly average to rise to $464.</p><p>State officials say people switched to plans with lower and more affordable premiums. For example, more than 37,000 people moved to “bronze level” plans, which tend to have higher deductibles but lower monthly payments. An even larger share in Illinois than in the past moved into gold plans with lower deductibles, which follows a trend happening for the last few years across the country. The state says gold plans were more affordable for Illinois residents this year because of premium alignment.</p><p>The switch to bronze plans mirrors a larger national trend.</p><p>“They’re sort of hedging their bets that they’re hopefully not going to have a medical emergency in a given year,” McGough said. “They’re just trying to maintain some form of health coverage even if it means that they could potentially be on the hook for more financially if they have to seek out medical services.”</p><h3></h3><h3>Digging deeper to find the real health care costs</h3><p>But to truly measure affordability, consumers need to think beyond the deductible and monthly premium, said Carrie Espinosa, owner of Horizon Benefit Services in north suburban Gurnee.</p><p>For example, she saw one plan this year that introduced a deductible for prescriptions, which could end up shifting — or increasing — how much a person ends up paying for health care over the year.</p><p>When Espinosa is advising people on plans, she tells them to evaluate if their doctors are in network, the cost of medications, office visits and what procedures or tests they may need each year.</p><p>“You want to consider all of those factors as you’re making your decision, but the driver in all of that, in my humble opinion, is affordability,” she said. “If I can’t afford the health plan, I’m not going to be able to afford to use it.”</p><p>Espinosa had a handful of clients who opted this year to become uninsured, but others found ways to adjust their income to remain eligible for some form of assistance through the ACA.</p><p>Experts and officials, in particular Democrats, had warned that the expired enhanced premium tax credits could push people to become uninsured. The fight over the enhanced tax credits is what led to last fall’s government shutdown.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ab0000" name="image-ab0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4c50401/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2Ff0%2Ff1ebe7c84837bf4ade39ed950e71%2Fmedicaid-stroger-0522250143.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fffe452/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2Ff0%2Ff1ebe7c84837bf4ade39ed950e71%2Fmedicaid-stroger-0522250143.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c1c572/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2Ff0%2Ff1ebe7c84837bf4ade39ed950e71%2Fmedicaid-stroger-0522250143.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="View outside the Professional Building of Cook County Health on May 22, 2025. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c1c572/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2Ff0%2Ff1ebe7c84837bf4ade39ed950e71%2Fmedicaid-stroger-0522250143.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/018fab1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2Ff0%2Ff1ebe7c84837bf4ade39ed950e71%2Fmedicaid-stroger-0522250143.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7c1c572/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2Ff0%2Ff1ebe7c84837bf4ade39ed950e71%2Fmedicaid-stroger-0522250143.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cook County Health has seen its uninsured patient population rise this year and faces an expected jump in uninsured patients next year as changes to the Medicaid public health insurance program begin to roll out.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Medical providers around Chicago say it’s too soon to determine if the expired tax credits are causing people to become uninsured, but some report seeing more uninsured patients and are generally hearing concerns about rising health care costs. Paul Dark, from Benefit Management Associates, said costs increased this year not just for those who get insurance through the ACA but also for those who get health insurance through an employer, with some plans rising by as much as 40%.</p><p>Cook County Health is among the institutions that have seen an increase in uninsured patients, from 21% last year to about 25% this year so far, said Dr. Erik Mikaitis, the chief executive officer. That’s grown since the enhanced tax credits for the ACA expired, but other factors, including a drop in patients with Medicaid public health insurance, could also be driving that change.</p><p>Mikaitis is also bracing for further changes to Medicaid that push more people off the program. The county recently launched a new website, <a class="Link" href="http://getmedicaidfacts.com" target="_blank" ><u>getmedicaidfacts.com</u></a>, to help sort through those changes.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dr. Mustafa Alavi is the medical director at the Erie Evanston/Skokie Health Center.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Dr. Mustafa Alavi, the medical director at the Erie Evanston/Skokie Health Center, sees patients falling through the cracks of both health care programs.</p><p>One patient lost Medicaid coverage because their income is too high, but they also can’t afford a plan through the ACA, Alavi said. The patient is now unable to maintain regular care to treat two chronic conditions. Another patient still has health insurance through the ACA but the costs have quadrupled, Alavi said. That makes it more difficult to buy medication.</p><p>“I had to prioritize which of the many priorities are important to address this year solely knowing that she now has more higher costs from her insurance plan,” Alavi said. “Not because it’s medically the right thing to do for my patients — it’s because I’m trying to make sure she can financially afford the medical care she needs.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-230000" name="html-embed-module-230000"></a><iframe class="airtable-embed" src="https://airtable.com/embed/appeodIS71ulzBjDz/pagn9KdFsVG6JvoEl/form" frameborder="0" onmousewheel="" width="100%" height="533" style="background: transparent; border: 1px solid #ccc;"></iframe> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: Pedestrian shot near Union Station nearly clipped by shooter's car, prompting road rage, death threat: police
entries.summary: <p>Road rage apparently sparked a man to jump from his car and race after a 42-year-old pedestrian, while screaming death threat moments before shooting him in the leg during the morning rush <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/23/man-42-shot-during-scuffle-near-union-station-in-west-loop" target="_blank" > near Union Station</a> Thursday morning in the West Loop, according to Chicago police. </p><p>The 42-year-old was walking on the sidewalk in the 500 block of West Van Buren Street around 8:30 a.m. when a black Chevrolet Malibu with Indiana plates nearly hit him while he was in the crosswalk, according to police reports obtained by the Sun-Times. </p><p>That caused the pedestrian to kick the car, causing the driver to jump out of the car, saying he was "going to kill him," the police reports stated. </p><p>But as the pedestrian began walking away from the area, the gunman, who was wearing a black hat and a black and white striped jumpsuit, parked on Canal Street and began following the 42-year-old before pulling out a handgun and shooting him in the left leg, police said. </p><p>The pedestrian, who could not be reached immediately by the Sun-Times, was taken to Stroger Hospital where he was in good condition.</p><p>Meanwhile, the shooter sped away in his car south on Canal Street, officials said. </p><p>Area 3 detectives continued to investigate. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

foreign policy.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mariah Woelfel/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-i/" target="_blank" >Some Chicagoans aren’t paying federal taxes in protest of Trump policies</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-d/" target="_blank" >Mariah Woelfel</a></h5><p><b>'War tax resistance'</b>: Organizers say they’re seeing a barrage of interest in the longstanding movement that involves not paying federal taxes. Some Chicagoans point to the government’s aggressive deportation efforts, the United States' support for Israel in the war in Gaza and the U.S.-Israel war against Iran as reasons for engaging in this kind of civil disobedience. </p><p><b>Getting organized</b>: "This definitely is our busiest tax season that I’ve witnessed," said Lincoln Rice, a Milwaukee organizer with the National War Tax Resistance Coordinating Committee, who hasn’t paid most of his federal taxes since 1998. The group provides training on tax resistance and consequences, which include threatening letters from the Internal Revenue Service, a monthly fine, and, in extreme cases, wage garnishment or prison time.</p><p><b>Zooming in</b>: Local attorney Rachel Cohen decided to not pay federal taxes, instead putting the owed amount into a high-yield savings account and paying only her state taxes. "I was thinking about my willingness to be hit with chemical weapons," Cohen said. "And I couldn’t square giving the federal government $10,000 with being willing to get arrested or put my body on the line."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-h/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>CANNABIS 💨</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-800000" name="image-800000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/127a214/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+547/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F43%2Fd3e7cc724d389528b5d67decb3d0%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-04.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fbb750d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+547/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F43%2Fd3e7cc724d389528b5d67decb3d0%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-04.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f2b495e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+547/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F43%2Fd3e7cc724d389528b5d67decb3d0%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-04.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="An employee shows off some flower at Zen Leaf Dispensary at 222 S Halsted St at Greektown, Thursday, April 23, 2026. class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-k/" target="_blank" >Trump administration’s shift on weed classification gives Illinois cannabis firms major tax break</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-u/" target="_blank" >Kade Heather</a></h5><p><b>The shift</b>: The Trump administration’s reclassification of medical marijuana will offer some Illinois weed companies a massive tax break.</p><p><b>Reclassification</b>: The order shifts state-licensed medical cannabis from Schedule I — meant for drugs without medical use and a high potential for abuse — to the more loosely regulated Schedule III. It also applies to cannabis products approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. The new policy offers significant tax relief by allowing medical marijuana operators to deduct business expenses on their federal taxes. It also lowers the barrier for more cannabis research.</p><p><b>All not well</b>: Reclassifying all types of cannabis isn’t going far enough to reform cannabis laws or to fund actual research, industry leaders said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>WRESTLING 🤼‍♂️</h4><div >Berwyn Eagles club will stop hosting wrestling, to fans’ dismay</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-n/" target="_blank" >Ambar Colón</a></h5><p><b>Final bow</b>: The operator of the beloved indie wrestling venue Berwyn Eagles Club says it will stop hosting shows after more than 20 years, leaving members of Chicago’s wrestling community stunned. The final events this weekend include a sold-out show Friday hosted by AAW Pro Wrestling and a lucha extravaganza from All Star Lucha Libre on Saturday.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Wrestling fans say the Eagles Club offered budget-friendly prices and free parking. Some of the world’s biggest wrestling stars have shed blood, sweat and tears in the west suburban venue, making it a cornerstone for a sport that combines athleticism, entertainment and sometimes raw brutality.</p><p><b>Fight night gone wrong</b>: The plans to end wrestling come after a fight broke out between an unnamed fan and a wrestler, Mads Krule Krügger, at a March 28 Ruthless Pro Wrestling show. Videos posted to social media showed wrestlers and attendees punching and kicking the fan.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-e/" target="_blank" >Cindy Hernandez</a></h5><h5></h5><p><b>Bookstore crawl</b>: Book lovers will have the chance to explore local shops across the Chicago area Saturday during the annual Chicagoland Bookstore Crawl, held on the national Independent Bookstore Day. Eighty-four stores are participating, double the number last year, organizers said.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Large book-selling retailers are continuing to expand in Chicago, including Barnes & Noble, which will open its largest location here in June. And on the West Side, they can enjoy a set by trumpeter Victor Garcia in Austin, or a retrospective of Chicago jazz at the Garfield Park Conservatory.</p><p>And that’s only a sample of shows on "Neighborhood Jazz Night," which is part of UNESCO International Jazz Day programming.</p><p>Chicago is a first-time host for the celebration, which culminates in the All-Star Global Concert on Thursday at Lyric Opera House. That performance will feature more than 40 artists including Herbie Hancock, Kurt Elling, Dianne Reeves and Dee Dee Bridgewater.</p><p>But the large-scale show only captures some of Chicago’s jazz scene. Happening all month, a wealth of events — many of them free — have been exploring the history of jazz in the city, honoring homegrown talent and supporting local venues.</p><p>See our list of Jazz Day highlights for must-attend events.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-ju/" target="_blank" ><b>JAZZ DAY HIGHLIGHTS HERE</b></a></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p> <br>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiuuujl-l-tr/" target="_blank" >suntimes.com/member</a>.</p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Sweets maker Ferrero launches new Franklin Park-made Nutella Peanut spread
entries.summary: <p>Ferrero North America launched the first Nutella flavor in more than 60 years, and it's exclusively made in the Chicago area. </p><p>The confectioner held a ribbon cutting Friday at its Franklin Park facility, where it invested $75 million to be able to produce the new product. </p><p>Nutella Peanut is the first-ever variant of Nutella since the hazelnut spread debuted in 1964. It also marks the first time a Nutella product has been manufactured in the U.S., according to the company.</p><p>The first shipment of Nutella Peanut — only available in North America — hit shelves earlier this month and can be found at major retailers like Walmart and Target.</p><p>The new flavor comes as the sweet-packaged food company continues to expand its North American presence with new products and acquisitions. Italy-based parent company Ferrero Group acquired cereal company WK Kellogg Co. last year for an estimated $3.1 billion. In 2017, it purchased Chicago-based <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/10/18/18349589/nutella-maker-soothes-sweet-tooth-buys-ferrara-candy" >Ferrara Candy Co.</a> The following year it bought Nestle’s U.S. candy brands, including Nerds, Butterfinger and Baby Ruth — which adorn Ferrero’s spinning sign along Interstate 294, marking where the candy bars are produced. And in 2022, it bought Wells Enterprises, the maker of ice cream brands like Blue Bunny and Halo Top</p><p>Michael Lindsey, president and chief business officer of Ferrero North America, said Nutella Peanut has been years in the making. </p><p>“America has a love affair with peanuts — not just in the South but across the whole country. So, we've been working on and trying to see how can we adapt the classic recipe of Nutella to incorporate natural peanuts from the U.S.,” Lindsey said. “We finally have a formula that we think is absolutely exceptional.”</p><p>He said there’s “really nothing like” Nutella Peanut in the market. The hazelnuts are sourced from Oregon and the peanuts from Georgia and other Southeast states.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-de0000" name="image-de0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7368fa4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F88%2Fbbe3a9c648e3abd4e11905ae9b27%2Fferrero-260424-31.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4274bb0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4b%2F88%2Fbbe3a9c648e3abd4e11905ae9b27%2Fferrero-260424-31.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A factory worker samples Ferrero’s new Nutella Peanut spread after it came off the assembly line at the company’s Franklin Park facility.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Nutella Peanut line is the first major expansion Ferrero has undertaken at its Franklin Park facility, since acquiring it from Nestle. The product created 50 jobs, spanning from production line packing to on-site supervisors and managers, according to Lindsey.</p><p>Many of those new roles are already filled, since production began a few months ago. But “there's always new opportunities being created,” he said.</p><p>The plant at 3401 Mt. Prospect Road was built in the 1960s.</p><p>“It's perfectly situated for us, right there off of the major freeways,” Lindsey said. “[It] produces a lot of our loved products already, so it was a fairly natural choice to expand it for Nutella Peanut.”</p><p>Ferrero has has more than 1,700 employees in Illinois, following recent acquisitions. </p><p>In 2021, it broke ground on a new $75 million chocolate processing facility in Bloomington, creating 50 jobs. About a year later, the company further invested in the city with a new $214 million Kinder Bueno production facility.</p><p>Ferrero’s Innovation Center and North America Research and Development Labs opened in the Loop’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/9/19/23881106/ferrero-celebrates-opening-innovation-center-chicago-loop-marshall-field" >Marshall Field and Co. building</a> in 2023, where it develops new confectionery and packaged food products, as well as existing brands such as Keebler and Famous Amos.</p><p>“This investment underscores why Illinois remains a strong place for advanced manufacturing,” Illinois Senate President Don Harmon, D-Oak Park, said in a news release. “Ferrero’s expansion in Franklin Park is creating good jobs, supporting local communities and proving that innovation and production can thrive right here in our state.”</p><p>Lindsey anticipates Nutella Peanut's manufacturing will eventually expand outside of Franklin Park. He said the decision to start locally wasn't driven by tariffs but Ferrero's overarching plans for North American investment that have been in place for years.</p><p>“I expect [Nutella Peanut] to be a huge hit, and I assume someone else in the world is going to want it at some point,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Lunchroom workers block Downtown street in protest as contract talks with CPS stall
entries.summary: <p>Sixty-one-year-old Kimberly Penson gets on the bus by 4:45 a.m. so she can make eggs, bagels or biscuits before the first bell rings for the elementary students she calls “her babies.”</p><p>And then, as the sole cook at her school, she turns to preparing lunch, barely sitting down until she leaves at 3 p.m.</p><p>Penson is part of the fabric of Greene Elementary School in McKinley Park. When former students see her out and about they often exclaim, “Oh, hey, Miss Kim!”</p><p>“They jump out of their cars and they hug me,” she said. “I think that's wonderful because they recognize who I am and what I did for them.”</p><p>But Penson, who has worked for Chicago Public Schools for 21 years and makes an annual salary of about $34,000, said she feels deeply disrespected by the school district’s leadership. She and her colleagues, who are represented by the union Unite Here Local 1, have been <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/10/27/cps-lunchroom-workers-wages-so-low-they-can-barely-feed-own-families-chicago-public-schools" target="_blank" ><u>negotiating a contract for more than 11 months</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-540000" name="image-540000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f781c5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F58%2F207250dd4e10be2fc8267d308d44%2Flunchroomunion-260424-7.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cbecb83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F58%2F207250dd4e10be2fc8267d308d44%2Flunchroomunion-260424-7.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Kim Penson, a 21 year lunch room worker veteran, stands outside Nathanael Greene Elementary School where she’s been working at for 9 years located at 3525 S. Honore St. in the McKinley Park neighborhood, Tuesday, April 21, 2026. | Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>To “sound the alarm” on what they see as unfair and unequal wages, about 250 workers and their supporters held a protest Thursday afternoon at which two dozen of them — including Penson — sat down on Madison Street outside the school district's downtown headquarters as an act of civil disobedience. Chicago police officers quickly detained the workers and escorted them away to cheers from their fellow union members, who later chanted "we will be back." The union said they were quickly released and ticketed for pedestrian traffic violations. </p><p>The rally started at Daley Plaza before workers wearing shirts that read, "We feed Chicago's children," marched to CPS' offices, where a monthly Board of Education meeting had let out.</p><p>The two biggest sticking points in contract talks, according to Unite Here: wages and staffing levels.</p><p>On Wednesday, a CPS spokesperson said both sides agreed earlier this month to call in a federal mediator, who typically intervenes when talks have stalled to prevent a strike.</p><p>“CPS remains committed to reaching a fair and sustainable agreement,” the spokesperson wrote in a statement. “A neutral third party will help ensure that negotiations proceed productively. CPS looks forward to working with the mediator to resolve outstanding issues in the best interest of our employees and the students we serve.”</p><p>Lunchroom workers, including cooks, porters who clean kitchens and attendants who serve food, are among the lowest paid full-time workers in the school district. Their average salaries range from about $23,400 to $37,400, according to CPS’ employee roster from last month. Federal poverty levels range from $15,960 for individuals to $33,000 for a family of four in 2026.</p><p>An analysis by the Chicago Sun-Times earlier this year identified CPS among the top 10 employers statewide in terms of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/04/23/lunchroom-workers-plan-to-block-traffic-as-contract-talks-with-cps-stall" >number of their workers who receive SNAP</a> food assistance. In a 2025 survey ahead of contract negotiations, Unite Here found that about 22% of lunchroom workers received SNAP benefits and had visited food banks.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-230000" name="image-230000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f9a28f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F30%2Fb61caefc47428fbc0234482ab01c%2Flunchroomunion-260424-004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/70bf3b8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F30%2Fb61caefc47428fbc0234482ab01c%2Flunchroomunion-260424-004.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bad1038/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F30%2Fb61caefc47428fbc0234482ab01c%2Flunchroomunion-260424-004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Two people chant at a protest in support of Chicago Public Schools lunchroom workers." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bad1038/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F30%2Fb61caefc47428fbc0234482ab01c%2Flunchroomunion-260424-004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dafc4ed/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F30%2Fb61caefc47428fbc0234482ab01c%2Flunchroomunion-260424-004.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bad1038/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F30%2Fb61caefc47428fbc0234482ab01c%2Flunchroomunion-260424-004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Public Schools lunchroom workers and supporters rally at Daley Plaza to demand higher wages, more staffing, and a renegotiated contract Thursday. | Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Times</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Patrick Griffin, organizing director for Unite Here, said its workers should be paid on par with other similarly situated workers in CPS who are represented by other unions.</p><p>In the latest CTU contract, the minimum earnings for teacher assistants and other support staff <a class="Link" href="https://www.ctulocal1.org/posts/full-salary-tables/" target="_blank" ><u>were raised to $40,000</u></a>. All full-time SEIU 73 members, which include security staff and special education teacher aides, earn a baseline salary of $40,000, according <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2024/05/03/cps-seiu-local-73-public-school-support-staff-contract-strike-teachers-2019" target="_blank" ><u>to their latest contract.</u></a></p><p>“We think that people should be treated equally,” Griffin said. “The people who work in the lunchrooms, their wages are so far behind everybody else.”</p><p>But CPS has so far refused to agree to that minimum for lunchroom workers. The cash-strapped district is projecting at least a $520 million deficit for the upcoming school year and has tried to avoid taking on additional costs.</p><p>At the same time, CPS has steadily decreased the number of lunchroom workers it employs. CPS has 20% fewer lunchroom attendants, cooks and porters on staff compared to five years ago, according to a WBEZ analysis. That amounts to 324 fewer positions,187 of which were lost in the past year.</p><p>Griffin said a lot of school cafeterias only have one or two workers, which means that if one person is out, the other has to rush to cook, hand out food and clean up solo.</p><p>“It's extremely stressful,” he said.</p><p>Union representatives have attended school board meetings in recent months to urge the district to sign a new contract, highlighting the financial stress lunchroom workers are under.</p><p>At a Board of Education meeting in February, Roushaunda Williams, who sits on the executive board for Unite Here, said she’d seen how her sister-in-law, a CPS lunchroom worker for more than 26 years, had struggled.</p><p>“Her heart is big, she loves the kids, but she’s seldomly validated,” Williams said.</p><p>In a survey conducted by the union last year, <a class="Link" href="https://www.unitehere1.org/wp-content/uploads/CPS-Lunchroom-Report-2025.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>88% of lunchroom workers who responded reported</u></a> having recent trouble paying for necessities such as food, housing, electricity, child care or medical needs, Williams noted.</p><p>“They can’t afford food,” Williams said. “Every day the board fails to settle a fair contract is another day that CPS lunchroom workers struggle to feed their families.”</p><p>CPS officials have said they are focused on delivering higher wages for workers. District officials told school board members in February they had proposed increases so no worker earns less than $20 dollars an hour by this August. That’s more than the city’s current $16.60 minimum wage and would translate to $41,600 a year for full-time workers.</p><p>Lunchroom workers are hourly workers who do not get paid during the summer or on winter breaks or holidays. Unite Here officials say they’d need to be paid $24.63 an hour to approach $40,000 per year.</p><p>The union said CPS’ most recent proposal would bring pay up to $19 an hour for the 2026-27 school year. The starting wage for lunchroom attendants now is $16.78 an hour, and the union has said the vast majority of lunchroom workers make less than $18.42 an hour.</p><p>Griffin said CPS’ proposals “don’t get our members out of poverty” and the two sides are still “far apart” on wages.</p><p>In Penson’s cafeteria, there was an additional lunchroom attendant five years ago. But with that help gone, Penson said the work has become even more strenuous. Her right foot recently became inflamed from the many hours she spends standing on the concrete floor, and she now has to do physical therapy twice a week.</p><p>The doctor told Penson to get specific shoes. But she said they are expensive, and she wants to give any extra money she has to her grandchildren. Just this week, she said, she helped them pay for a field trip.</p><p>The rent in her Englewood apartment keeps increasing, but her salary isn’t keeping up. At times, she said she has so little food in her own kitchen that she goes to her mother’s or her sister’s to get a meal.</p><p>Her eyes light up at the prospect of getting paid $40,000 a year. It would be an almost 18% raise.</p><p>“That would be big,” she said. “I can do for my grandkids.”</p><p><b><i>Contributing:</i></b><i> Elvia Malagón and Lauren FitzPatrick</i></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Former Bull Ayo Dosunmu talks TWolves, free agency, return to Chicago?
entries.summary: <p>MINNEAPOLIS — For months, every entry in Ayo Dosunmu’s journal included the same five words: “We will make the playoffs.”</p><p>It might be at the beginning as he was first jotting down his thoughts. It might be the closing line. But starting last September during Bulls training camp, Dosunmu didn’t miss a day, almost hellbent on writing into existence what had become his personal mantra.</p><p>On Thursday, he and his teammates went through their morning shootaround, preparing for Game 3 of their best-of-seven first-round playoff series.</p><p>Dosunmu had made the playoffs, all right . . . with the Timberwolves.</p><p>“The more and more I’m here, the more opportunity for spotlight games, the first [playoffs] since my rookie year and being a monumental piece to help the team win — you can’t ask for anything better than that,” the former Illinois guard, now in his fifth NBA season, told the Sun-Times. “It’s a blessing in disguise, actually. Play-ing meaningful basketball, playing in the playoffs, that’s always been a dream of mine, and that’s why I wrote that in my journal every day. God just sent me here with a different team.”</p><p>Dosunmu and forward Julian Phillips were traded to Minnesota on Feb. 4, one day before the deadline, for guard Rob Dillingham, forward Leonard Miller and four second-round draft picks It was one of seven deals the Bulls made leading up to the deadline as Arturas Karnisovas then the executive vice president of basketball operations, and then-general manager Marc Eversley purged the roster of Dosunmu, Phillips, center Nikola Vucevic, guards Coby White and Jevon Carter and guard/forwards Kevin Huerter and Dalen Terry.</p><p>“I wouldn’t say I was surprised because I understood the circumstances with my contract and how many expiring contracts we had, so I knew it was a possibility,” Dosunmu said. “I’d say from the beginning of the season, I told myself I wouldn’t let that get into my play, get into the team, so I was going to never think about it.</p><p>“But as the trade deadline approached, 24, 48 hours before the trade deadline, you started to see the moves, and I understood the possibility could become bigger.”</p><p>It did, of course. And the fallout from the trades became one of the many reasons why Karnisovas and Eversley were fired before the end of the regular season.</p><p>Dosunmu said he recently spoke with Karnisovas, the man who drafted him in 2021 to play for his hometown team, and found some closure.</p><p>“Sometimes you have that thankfulness,” he said. “You’re drafted somewhere, you help a player develop, help a player take it to another step, and that’s why I love Chicago so much, because I was a second-round pick, fought my way into the rotation, got better each and every year, and that was my home. So when I was dealt, that kind of also put a chip on my shoulder because I felt like they just threw away all the work they put into me.</p><p>“I was able to close that chapter, speak with [Karnisovas]. He said what he said, I said what I said, and we move on from there. It’s a business, and I wish those guys the best of luck, wish [departing coach] Billy [Donovan] the best of luck. Now [I’m] focused on trying to get far in the playoffs.”</p><p>He also aims to show the rest of the NBA that he’s more than the player they saw in Chicago — that he can be a difference-maker for a talented postseason team. He was making a good argument considering his team-high 25 points in the Game 3 win over Denver.</p><p>He’ll be a free agent in July. Tim Connelly, the Timberwolves’ president of basketball operations, has told Dosunmu he wants Minnesota to be his home, but the T-wolves also have $188 million committed to next season’s payroll and are up against the first salary-tax apron.</p><p>Would Dosunmu consider a return to the Bulls?</p><p>“Everything is on the table when you’re a free agent,” he said. “That’s the main thing about being a free agent — you listen. You have conversations with whoever is interested. I’ll sit down with my family, and you have those conversations about where the next duration of my career will be, but Minnesota has shown me love here. I love the love I’ve been getting from the coaching staff, the fans, the players. So of course Minnesota will have the first dibs to do right and make me feel at home where I won’t even have to go out and entertain [other offers].</p><p>“This is what free agency is all about, sitting down and talking to teams, so I wouldn’t rule out no team. But that’s down the road.”</p><p>A journal entry for another day.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Trabajadores de cafeterías de CPS bloquean la calle en protesta mientras negociaciones se estancan
entries.summary: <p>La señora Kimberly Penson, de 61 años, se sube al autobús a las 4:45 a.m. para ir a hacer huevos, bagels o galletas antes de que suene la primera campana para los estudiantes de primaria que ella llama “sus bebés”.</p><p>Y luego, como la única cocinera de su escuela, se dedica a preparar el almuerzo, apenas sentándose hasta que se va a las 3 p.m.</p><p>Penson es parte de la esencia de la Escuela Primaria Greene en McKinley Park. Cuando antiguos alumnos la ven por ahí, a menudo exclaman, “¡Hola, Miss Kim!”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d90000" name="module-d90000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Saltan de sus carros y me abrazan”, dijo. “Creo que es maravilloso porque reconocen quién soy y lo que hice por ellos”.</p><p>Pero Penson, quien ha trabajado para las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) durante 21 años y tiene un salario anual de alrededor de $34,000, dijo que se siente profundamente menospreciada por el liderazgo del distrito escolar.</p><p>Ella y sus colegas, que están representados por el sindicato Unite Here Local 1, han estado <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/10/27/cps-lunchroom-workers-wages-so-low-they-can-barely-feed-own-families-chicago-public-schools" target="_blank" >negociando un contrato durante más de 11 meses</a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6fd798" name="image-6fd798"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
